items.title: I like to talk about stocks more , when they offer larger margin of safety to longer term averages , I don't like to talk about it when they are at 20...
items.description_html:
I like to talk about stocks more , when they offer larger margin of safety to longer term averages , I don't like to talk about it when they are at 20 times book or sales and are clearly overvalued and the hot theme

Hence when I tweet about a company today for some it is boring , but 1 year later I see so much interest in the same stock , to stay ahead of the curve reading through companies that are good companies in tough times is very important and to maintain that discipline to chase good valuations and good growth , not peak margins and peak multiples

Entry valuations is one of the single most important controllable factor , use it to make the equation in your favor !

items.description_html:
Climate change could expand the reach of chikungunya into parts of North America, Europe and East Asia by the end of the century, a new study warns.
A new modelling study says warming temperatures could allow disease-carrying mosquitoes to establish hotspots in regions that were previously too cold for sustained transmission.
Countries in temperate zones must strengthen mosquito surveillance and healthcare preparedness before 2040, writes @himanshujourno

https://www.downtoearth.org.in/climate-change/climate-change-may-expand-chikungunya-risk-in-north-america-europe-and-asia-study-says

items.title: This isn't a chip cycle. It's a buildout. Every prior semiconductor boom ran on inventory restocking or the next phone refresh, and peaked within a fe...
items.description_html:
This isn't a chip cycle. It's a buildout.

Every prior semiconductor boom ran on inventory restocking or the next phone refresh, and peaked within a few quarters. This one is different.

Chipmaker revenue, Nasdaq semis, and Korea's KOSPI (home to SK Hynix and Samsung) are now moving as one. Memory prices are surging, proof the AI wave has spread past GPUs into high-bandwidth memory, DDR5, and enterprise SSDs, tightening the very segments that were oversupplied a year ago.

The market isn't pricing a cycle that peaks in quarters. It's pricing an infrastructure buildout that runs for years.

items.title: RT Danny (Dennis) Citrinowicz ,داني سيترينوفيتش: השאלה המרכזית כעת היא לא האם תהיה הסלמה, אלא האם...
items.description_html:
RT Danny (Dennis) Citrinowicz ,داني سيترينوفيتش
השאלה המרכזית כעת היא לא האם תהיה הסלמה, אלא האם ניתן יהיה לעצור אותה לאחר התגובה האיראנית. זה תלוי בעיקר בגורם אחד, קרי ביכולתו וברצונו של טראמפ לכפות על ישראל לעצור לאחר התגובה האיראנית. קשה לראות את איראן של היום מבליגה על תקיפות ישראליות בשטחה, במיוחד כאשר מבחינתה מדובר בפגיעה ישירה בריבונותה ובמעמדה האזורי.

מנקודת המבט האיראנית, האירועים הנוכחיים חורגים בהרבה מהעימות הנקודתי. הם נועדו לעצב את המשוואה העתידית בכל הנוגע לפעילות הישראלית בלבנון ובמרחב האזורי בכלל. מבחינת טהראן, אם ישראל תוכל לפעול נגד נכסים איראניים או נגד בעלי בריתה ללא תגובה, הדבר יפגע באופן מהותי בהרתעה האיראנית. לכן גם תגובה ישראלית עוצמתית לא צפויה להביא את איראן לסגת מהקו שהיא מבקשת לקבוע.

טראמפ אינו מעוניין בהסלמה אזורית רחבה, בוודאי לא כאשר מחירי הנפט מגיבים בעליות וכל עימות נוסף עלול לערער את היציבות הכלכלית והביטחונית באזור. עם זאת, יכולת ההשפעה שלו על ישראל גדולה לאין שיעור מיכולתו להשפיע על ההנהגה האיראנית הנוכחית. בעוד שלארצות הברית יש מנופי לחץ משמעותיים מול ירושלים, יכולתה לשנות את תפיסת העולם האסטרטגית של טהראן מוגבלת הרבה יותר.

לכן, אם טראמפ לא יציב גבולות ברורים ולא ידרוש עצירה לאחר התגובה האיראנית, סביר להניח שישראל תמשיך בפעולותיה. במקרה כזה, הסיכוי להידרדרות לסבב הסלמה נוסף ואף ממושך יגדל באופן משמעותי.


Avner Vilan: דניס נותן פה את הגרסא מדוע אנחנו הולכים לקראת הסלמה. הוא קצת מתעלם מהעובדה שדרושים שניים לטנגו וטראמפ לא רוצה התדרדרות בלתי נשלטת אבל שווה לקרוא.

items.description_html:
Andaman and Nicobar Islands now have India’s lowest fertility rate, according to NFHS-6.
The Union Territory’s total fertility rate has fallen to 0.9 — less than half the replacement rate of 2.1 — signalling a sharp demographic shift.
The data also shows fewer young children and a growing elderly population, with experts linking the decline to smaller family preferences, better access to family planning, women’s literacy, education and economic independence.
@bsrivas explains what this means for India’s changing population story.
https://www.downtoearth.org.in/health/andaman-and-nicobar-islands-record-indias-lowest-fertility-rate-in-nfhs-6

items.title: Container shipping rates are surging amid the Iran War: The spot rate for a 40-foot container from Asia to the US West Coast jumped +$655, or +20%, ov...
items.description_html:
Container shipping rates are surging amid the Iran War:

The spot rate for a 40-foot container from Asia to the US West Coast jumped +$655, or +20%, over the last week, to ~$3,933, the highest in at least 6 months.

At the same time, the rate from Asia to Northern Europe jumped +$793, or +27%, to ~$3,649.

Since the start of the Iran War, Asia-to-US container rates have surged +109%, and Asia-to-Europe rates are up by more than +50%.

This comes as shipments rerouted around the blocked Strait of Hormuz are causing severe congestion at Southeast Asian transshipment hubs, including Singapore and Malaysia.

On top of this, carriers are charging importers fuel surcharges and shifting costs, while a pickup in demand heading into peak season for booking ocean freight is adding further pressure.

Looking ahead, available shipping capacity is set to shrink further as importers rush to replenish inventories in July and August, which could push rates even higher.

Supply chain pressures are intensifying.

items.title: In April 2023, Laurus Labs was deeply out of favour. COVID-linked revenues had disappeared. Margins had compressed sharply. The stock had fallen signi...
items.description_html:
In April 2023, Laurus Labs was deeply out of favour.

COVID-linked revenues had disappeared. Margins had compressed sharply. The stock had fallen significantly from its peak, and the prevailing view was that the growth story was over.

What stood out in Dr Satyanarayana Chava's interview was not any particular answer.

It was his demeanour.

There was no urgency to justify the numbers. No attempt to manufacture optimism. No visible concern about market sentiment.

He appeared focused on capabilities, capacity, customer relationships and the next phase of growth.

The market was focused on what had happened or what was about to happen in FY24. Management appeared focused on what was being built.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ms-BPf-EMyw&t=2397s

items.title: I’ve tested nearly every major AI model using companies I understand deeply. The moment you push back on weak or superficial analysis, the model ofte...
items.description_html:
I’ve tested nearly every major AI model using companies I understand deeply. The moment you push back on weak or superficial analysis, the model often concedes.

That’s precisely why I keep emphasising: don’t outsource your critical thinking to these systems. They are improving rapidly, but they are far from infallible. Think for yourself.

Do your own research. Read the annual reports (many FY26 reports are already out). Review Q4 FY26 earnings transcripts verbatim. Even better: listen to the audio recordings of the earnings calls. Investing is effort-intensive, but the rewards for intellectual curiosity are well worth it. Engineering and Pharma may surprise the bulls and humiliate the bears.

More Americans are stuck without work for longer:

The number of Americans unemployed for 27+ weeks rose by +155,000 in May to 1.99 million, the highest since December 2021.

YoY, this figure has surged +524,000, the largest annual increase since August 2021.

As a % of unemployment, this metric is up to 27.5%, the highest since December 2021.

This percentage has risen +9.0 points since the start of 2023 and is now above every post-recession peak, excluding 2008 and 2020.

An increasing number of Americans are struggling to find employment.

items.title: History of throwing eggs (What’s happening in Bengal) : The practice of throwing eggs at unpopular politicians/disliked public figures originated in ...
items.description_html:
History of throwing eggs (What’s happening in Bengal) :
The practice of throwing eggs at unpopular politicians/disliked public figures originated in 16th-century Europe. It was the ultimate symbol of public humiliation, driven by 3 factors:
•Eggs were a widely accessible, inexpensive everyday staple.
•They could heavily stain clothing & create a foul smell without causing fatal injuries.
•Audiences at theatrical performances/public stocks (pillories) could easily carry eggs to show their immediate disapproval of bad actors or criminals.
Over time, throwing eggs at politicians evolved into a recognized, albeit illegal, form of protest globally.

items.title: Demand for protection against a South Korean stock market decline is surging: Put option open interest in the South Korea ETF, $EWY, is up to ~880,000...
items.description_html:
Demand for protection against a South Korean stock market decline is surging:

Put option open interest in the South Korea ETF, $EWY, is up to ~880,000 contracts, the highest in at least 12 months.

The total number of outstanding put option contracts has QUADRUPLED over the last several weeks.

By comparison, call option open interest has increased +80,000 over the same period, to ~400,000 contracts, near the highest in a year.

To put this into perspective, both put and call option open interest stood at ~50,000 contracts in January 2026.

Meanwhile, $EWY declined -14% on Friday, posting its largest daily drop since the 2020 pandemic crash.

Investors are rushing to hedge against a correction in South Korea.

items.title: One more borderline racist opinion piece from @TheEconomist ...... here making fun of the Indian "uncle". Now, north Indians like making fun of the pr...
items.description_html: One more borderline racist opinion piece from @TheEconomist ...... here making fun of the Indian "uncle". Now, north Indians like making fun of the proverbial "phuphaji" - the bane of every family wedding. In Bengal, it is the eternally annoyed "pishi". I am sure other parts of India have their equivalent. However, the Economist is trying to convert this into some sort of political Gen Z thing by mocking the older generation. This is a serious cultural misunderstanding. We make fun of "phupha-ji" as a default - and this was true of all previous generations. Even phuphajis make fun of phuphajis. The journalist at Economist is trying to force fit their "angry, white male" stereotype that is a staple of the Western Left in their home market; tone deaf and contrived when applied to India.

items.title: RT Prashant Nair: Nasdaq Closed -4% On Friday ! A few thoughts, 1. Remember the backdrop when you look at Friday's sell off. Moves have been extreme. ...
items.description_html:
RT Prashant Nair
Nasdaq Closed -4% On Friday !

A few thoughts,

1. Remember the backdrop when you look at Friday's sell off. Moves have been extreme. The SOX (Semiconductor Sector Index) closed 75% above its 200-day MA on Wednesday. This is the most ‘extended’ above its 200-day MA since '99/'00 era !

2. This is equally interesting.. While the S&P 500 ended -2.6% on Friday. The S&P 500 ex-AI was unchanged. For context - the Mag7 index was down 3.7%.

3. US jobs data came in very strong. The 10y bond yield jumped 6 bps. The 2y was up 11 bps. Hotter jobs data risk could mean a longer Fed pause (some are calling for actual rate hikes but unlikely I think)

4. What also did not help were the Broadcom numbers (stock -20% in 2 days) & the Anthropic Recursive Learning headlines !

5. There continues to be increasing focus on equity market supply/issuance & the ability of the market to handle it ! Google equity issuance on Wednesday is just the beginning !

#Nifty #BankNifty #IranWar‌ #India #Oil #Rupee #RBI #Investing

@CNBCTV18News

items.title: 25% portfolio allocation in 2006 was down 50% within weeks. Capacity to suffer was the consulting income. That paid the bills. Stock not only recovere...
items.description_html:
25% portfolio allocation in 2006 was down 50% within weeks. Capacity to suffer was the consulting income. That paid the bills. Stock not only recovered, but went up 10x from previous high.

Same cycle repeated in 2009 - that was a blanket sale across the portfolio. Again the operating cash flow (salary) gave the immunity. You have to be seriously thick skinned to realise large gains on large allocations over ridiculously long holding periods.

Munger repeatedly said - big money is not in buying or selling, but waiting. People simply cannot wait in an always online, digital, dopamine rich environment!

items.title: Conflict in the backyard | Jharkhand In Jharkhand’s tribal villages, one night of elephant raids can wipe out months of farm work. Across Latehar, Kh...
items.description_html:
Conflict in the backyard | Jharkhand

In Jharkhand’s tribal villages, one night of elephant raids can wipe out months of farm work.

Across Latehar, Khunti, Palamu and beyond, small and marginal farmers say elephants are destroying paddy, potatoes, stored grain and forest produce — leaving families with little to survive on.

Low compensation, delayed payments and complex claims are deepening the crisis.

Niraj Sinha writes on how rising human-wildlife conflict is reshaping farming lives in Jharkhand.

https://www.downtoearth.org.in/agriculture/conflict-in-the-backyard-in-jharkhand-a-single-night-of-elephant-raids-can-wipe-out-months-of-farm-work

items.title: RT Prasanna Viswanathan: Karnataka CET 2026. Last year, 7 of top 10 CET engineering rankers were CBSE students. This year: 9 of top 10 are Karnataka s...
items.description_html:
RT Prasanna Viswanathan
Karnataka CET 2026.

Last year, 7 of top 10 CET engineering rankers were CBSE students. This year: 9 of top 10 are Karnataka state board. In the top 100, state board went from 54 to 79. CBSE collapsed from 45 to 19.

This is definitely not about state board students suddenly getting smarter. This is a about CBSE's botched systems directly repricing students out of competitive rankings they earned in classrooms.

Even as revaluation marks are still awaited , KEA has already said those marks won't be considered for CET ranks regardless. Meaning students who were incorrectly marked will carry incorrect ranks into college admissions. No correction mechanism.

CBSE students of 2026 are paying the price for an administrative failure they had zero part in creating.

items.title: One of the companies that has disappointed investors big time is "Krsnaa Diagnostics" It had a strong narrative at the time of listing Healthcare oppo...
items.description_html:
One of the companies that has disappointed investors big time is "Krsnaa Diagnostics"

It had a strong narrative at the time of listing

Healthcare opportunity was large, penetration was low, PPP model looked scalable & the company had presence across multiple states

But over time, the gap between opportunity size & actual execution became very clear

FY25 expectation was 25% growth, but actual growth came at 16%

FY26 expectation was 16% growth, but the company ended with low single digit

This is exactly why tracking results & concalls every quarter is so important

We used to hold a position earlier, but exited when execution was not coming through

When execution consistently falls short, it is better to exit rather than keep holding only because the story looks good

items.title: Garware over the years has quietly built something that has made it stand apart from all its domestic peers and not just domestic but even globally Ga...
items.description_html:
Garware over the years has quietly built something that has made it stand apart from all its domestic peers and not just domestic but even globally Garware is highly competitive both in terms of cost and quality.

It is the only domestic player manufacturing both SCF and PPF. Cosmo Films just entered in FY26. Jindal/Uflex don't make either, and 3M imports.

And the margin profile reflects the moat Garware has built, where it runs on a 22-25% margin profile. Cosmo in the 10-13% range and 3M India sits at 18-22% margins. Eastman globally does 19% but is levered.

And now there is this D2C pivot that the company is building. First, none of its peers currently have D2C and Garware is building this business in 3 layers -

> 250+ Garware Application Studios in India (targeting 300 shortly)
> 11 Global Application Studios (UAE, US)
> Garware Home Solutions - 6 studios live, targeting 50 by FY27-end

D2C margins run 25–30% higher than distributor margins. Currently ~10–15% of revenue globally, ~40% in India PPF.

When your SCF line produces 125 lakh sq ft/month vs PPF at 25 lakh sq ft/month, and the line design itself is proprietary with embedded automation and robotics, you've built a manufacturing moat that can't be reverse-engineered by throwing capex at the problem.

New 191Cr sun control line (30% capacity addition) > commercial production Q1 FY28.

The rationale? SCF at 75–80% utilization, hitting full by Q2 FY27 and architectural demand is exploding. Also, these lines are always fungible for the company which they can also use for PPF when demand shifts there.

They are also doing backward integration in TPU. Another lever for margin expansion. This line is commissioning by Oct 2026.

Garware over the years has built a business that is now the only domestic manufacturer, has proprietary line design, is backward integrated, has D2C distribution being built ground-up, a debt-free balance sheet funding growth, and a management team that navigated COVID, Russia, 50% US tariffs, and Middle East conflict without losing a single customer.

Disclaimer - not a recommendation to buy/sell

items.title: RT Derek Andersen: Here is my story about Vinod Khosla. In May 2017, I was six weeks from running out of cash. I had over extended myself funding a so...
items.description_html:
RT Derek Andersen
Here is my story about Vinod Khosla. In May 2017, I was six weeks from running out of cash. I had over extended myself funding a software project inside of Startup Grind that later became Bevy. One night at midnight on the couch I said to my wife, "I'm about to run out of money and we're going to lose everything," She said, "You'll figure it out." I stayed up all night sending emails and praying.

I had interviewed Vinod a few times at SG and barely knew him but I emailed him at 1:39am. He emailed me back at 7:34am asking for my number. He ended up calling me later that day in a ride to a meeting. His advice and encouragement helped propel me to raising $1M in 6 weeks and saved the company. I will never forget his willingness to answer an immediate call from a near stranger in my most desperate professional moment.

With US Fed raising interest rates

Inflation was inching higher thanks to oil and Iran war

items.title: RT Shereen Bhan: #IranUpdate US CentCom says Iran fired seven ballistic missiles toward Kuwait and Bahrain. ‘The missile were fired hours after U.S. ...
items.description_html:
RT Shereen Bhan
#IranUpdate US CentCom says Iran fired seven ballistic missiles toward Kuwait and Bahrain.

‘The missile were fired hours after U.S. Central Command shot down four Iranian one-way attack drones that were launched toward the Strait of Hormuz. The attack drones posed an immediate threat to regional maritime traffic. U.S. forces subsequently struck Iranian coastal surveillance radar sites in Goruk and on Qeshm Island to defend against further maritime attacks.

Initial assessments indicate six of the missiles launched by Iran were intercepted and a seventh did not reach its intended target. There are currently no reports of harm to U.S. personnel, and Iranian claims of damaging U.S. 5th fleet headquarters in Bahrain are false’ @CNBCTV18News @CNBCTV18Live #Iran #Bahrain #Kuwait #CNBCTV18Market

items.title: RT Gregory Brew: Some potentially very important takeaways from the @CNN interview with Mohsen Rezaei. -Rezaei frames the release of $12 billion in fr...
items.description_html:
RT Gregory Brew
Some potentially very important takeaways from the @CNN interview with Mohsen Rezaei.

-Rezaei frames the release of $12 billion in frozen funds (which other sources indicate is a key sticking point in discussions) as a confidence building move, rather than as compensation.

-he says that Iran is prepared for a resumption of hostilities but indicates the risks of war appear low (he also says that the IRI won the war, which he frames as a first).

-he does not indicate Iran will charge per ship tolls; a "maintenance fee" will be charged to cover the expenses of managing the waterway. How and by whom is unclear--payment by shippers to the IRGC has been framed by the US as a violation of US sanctions.
https://edition.cnn.com/2026/06/05/middleeast/iran-supreme-leader-adviser-mohsen-rezaei-interview-intl

items.description_html:
On World Environment Day, the question is not only how India builds more, but how it builds better.

Bio-composites such as Enwud, made from coconut coir fibres and wood residues, offer an alternative to timber and plywood in interiors, furniture, partitions and panels.

Manali Joshi writes that such materials can reduce pressure on forests, support circular manufacturing and help create cooler, more resilient cities.

https://www.downtoearth.org.in/environment/world-environment-day-indias-rise-of-bio-composites-offers-a-way-to-build-without-deforesting

items.title: RT CNBC-TV18: 🚨 The R&D Red Flag: Is Corporate India Underinvesting in Innovation? Veteran banker Uday Kotak says Google's $80 billion fund-raising...
items.description_html:
RT CNBC-TV18
🚨 The R&D Red Flag: Is Corporate India Underinvesting in Innovation?

Veteran banker Uday Kotak says Google's $80 billion fund-raising plan should be a wake-up call for India Inc — and a reminder that the future belongs to those who invest in it.

📊 The numbers tell a striking story:

🔹 India's private sector contributes just 35% of total R&D spending
🔹 The government continues to do most of the heavy lifting
🔹 In the USA, China and South Korea, private companies fund roughly 70-75% of R&D

🤔 The big question:

Should the government be carrying the innovation burden?

Or can fierce competition and capitalism at their best spur Corporate India to invest more aggressively in research, technology and the future?

👇 Who should drive India's innovation engine — government, business, or both?

@ShereenBhan @Reematendulkar
#IndiaInc #UdayKotak #RAndD #Innovation #Technology #Research #Google #IndiaEconomy #Business #CorporateIndia #FutureReady #EconomicGrowth #InvestingInInnovation #MakeInIndia #DeepTech #AI #Startups

items.title: RT Matthew Prince 🌥: Re One more I forgot until just reminded: 3. Khosla Ventures wanted to invest in our Series C. Vinod took me, Michelle, and Le...
items.description_html:
RT Matthew Prince 🌥
Re One more I forgot until just reminded:

3. Khosla Ventures wanted to invest in our Series C. Vinod took me, Michelle, and Lee out to dinner after he’d given us a term sheet. Near the end, Michelle and Lee got up to use the restroom. Vinod leaned over and said: “I’m impressed with you, not so much with them, what if you fire them and I’ll give you all their stock?” I think the charitable read was it was a test of my character. But I was so offended that we never spoke again. Literally blocked his number.

items.title: RT Hardeep Singh Puri: Congratulations @OilIndiaLimited ! An ocean of energy opportunities reinforced in the Andaman Sea! Very happy to report the pre...
items.description_html:
RT Hardeep Singh Puri
Congratulations @OilIndiaLimited !

An ocean of energy opportunities reinforced in the Andaman Sea!
Very happy to report the presence of natural gas in Sri Vijayapuram-3 an exploratory well drilled by Oil India Ltd. 15 km off the east coast of the Andaman Islands at a water depth of 355 meters.
Initial production testing of the well at the depth of 1900 plus meters in the Eocene formation has established the presence of natural gas through continuous flaring.
Oil India is carrying out gas sampling to assess the composition & calorific value of gas and to carry out isotope studies to understand the genesis of the gas.
Under the Samudra Manthan Mission (National Deep Water Exploration Mission) announced by Hon’ble PM @narendramodi Ji on Independence Day 2025, large number of deepwater & Ultra deepwater exploration wells are planned in our offshore basins to fully exploit our hydrocarbon reserves.
Presence of hydrocarbon is now reported in 2(Two) wells out of 3 (Three) exploratory wells drilled by OIL in current exploratory campaign off Andaman Basin.
This presence of natural gas will help us in taking forward our exploration ambitions in coordination with global deepwater exploration experts like @petrobras, @TotalEnergies, @bp_india, @Shell, @exxonmobil and will be a significant milestone in our journey through Amrit Kaal!
@PMOIndia @PIB_India @PetroleumMin @mygovindia
#OilIndia #EnergySecurity #Exploration #NaturalGas #Andaman

RT Nithin Kamath
Indian investors keep getting regular reminders that ignoring corporate governance can come back to bite them in spectacular fashion. A bad stock pick and poor position sizing can wipe out a big chunk of your portfolio.

Avoiding the worst of the worst stocks is perhaps more important than choosing the best ones. @Tijori1 has two useful features that can help you screen out terrible stocks.

On Tijoristack, you can generate a Risk Probe Report that shows you all the risks, red flags, and vulnerabilities you should watch out for. This is a detailed report that analyzes all the company financials and disclosures.

The second is the forensics tab on their site that gives you a broad overview of things you should dig deeper into.

Tijori website: https://www.tijorifinance.com
Tijoristack: https://www.tijoristack.ai/

Jefferies on Ambuja Cements (TP: ₹595)

Post meeting management at the Adani Conference

1⃣Acknowledged recent underperformance; integration of Sanghi, Penna & Orient has taken longer than expected.

2⃣ Next leg of value creation to come from execution, utilisation and cost efficiency, not aggressive capacity additions.

3⃣ Capacity to increase from 109 mtpa to 119 mtpa; further expansion on hold until returns improve. Earlier 140 MTPA target to be revisited.

4⃣ Reiterated cost-saving target of ~₹250/ton annually in FY27 & FY28, led by green power, logistics and operating efficiencies.

Jefferies says: Visible cost turnaround remains key to rebuilding investor confidence

items.title: On World Environment Day today, India’s clean air challenge is no longer just about measuring pollution — it is about responding when the air turns ...
items.description_html:
On World Environment Day today, India’s clean air challenge is no longer just about measuring pollution — it is about responding when the air turns dangerous.

India can now measure its air in real time. The Sameer app and the National Air Quality Index have made pollution levels visible to citizens. But data alone does not protect people.

Urvashi Prasad and Palak Mahajan of Pahle India Foundation argue that India’s next clean air challenge is not another dashboard, but a health response system that moves when pollution levels rise.

That means clear protocols for schools, clinics, district administrations, frontline health workers and care programmes — especially for those most at risk.

https://www.downtoearth.org.in/environment/world-environment-day-india-can-measure-its-air-now-it-must-learn-how-to-respond

#WorldEnvironmentDay #AirPollution #PublicHealth #SameerApp #AQI #CleanAir #ClimateChange #India #EnvironmentalHealth #PahleIndiaFoundation

items.title: RBI policy goes smoothly. - No rate hike; no change in stance. Higher inflation forecast sets the stage for rate hikes starting August or October base...
items.description_html:
RBI policy goes smoothly.

- No rate hike; no change in stance. Higher inflation forecast sets the stage for rate hikes starting August or October based on.

- Full package of dollar inflow measures: Subsidised FCNR; overseas borrowings (don't understand why only PSUs though - should have been ownership agnostic)

-Capital gains removed for FPIs in debt, along with a widening of pool of securities with no investment limit restrictions.

- Exporters asked to bring back proceeds quicker (timeline had been unusually lengthened during the trade crisis)

[Not one to blow our own trumpet, but hugely relieved that Reuters has got key stories right - that dollar inflow measures are coming and that rate hikes are not (immediately). Despite pressure from competition and markets!]

https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/indian-central-bank-keeps-key-policy-rate-hold-despite-falling-currency-2026-06-05/

items.description_html:
AXIS BANK DAYS HIGH

Axis Bank management mtg – targeting loan growth of ~300bps above industry growth over the medium term, supported by corporate + retail segments.. Retail disbursements have gained traction across secured + unsecured products.. fee income exp to grow in-line with assets..

Bank expects NIM of ~3.8% through cycle despite change in loan mix.. Total Income growth to outpace opex growth in FY27.. ECL impact exp to be minimal.. improving profitability metrics to drive RoA to ~1.6% in FY27E.. maintain BUY; TP 1630; 30% Upside @ 1.8x fy28e ABV

Brokerage Note

items.title: Bloomberg used indian prices to value rbi gold. Rbi hasnt changed to Indian prices yet , so they used lbma prices to value their gold and converted to...
items.description_html:
Bloomberg used indian prices to value rbi gold. Rbi hasnt changed to Indian prices yet , so they used lbma prices to value their gold and converted to rupees. Gold price in rupee went up 10% in may when the govt increased gold duty.

Since rbi releases the rupee and dollar value of gold every week, Bloomberg used to rupee value and Indian prices to figure out how much gold they have and assumed they've sold. Measure in dollars with Lbma prices, there would have been no sale detected.

Just explaining what's happened.

items.title: 🚨 Amazon pioneered e-commerce globally, but local startups like Flipkart were often ahead when it came to building an India-specific playbook. This...
items.description_html:
🚨 Amazon pioneered e-commerce globally, but local startups like Flipkart were often ahead when it came to building an India-specific playbook.

This included innovations such as cash on delivery (CoD), in-house logistics networks, and financing options like no-cost EMIs and debit card EMIs, many of which were popularised by Flipkart.

Amazon, however, has consistently proven to be a fast follower - quickly adapting successful ideas and scaling them aggressively.

A similar dynamic is now playing out in quick commerce, although it is still early days as Amazon gears up to challenge market leader Blinkit.

Amazon has reportedly committed more than $300 million to the business and has already opened 400–500 dark stores in key metros.

So why is quick commerce strategically important for Amazon? And what advantages, if any, does it have over Blinkit?

@AditiS90 has a detailed article

https://www.thearcweb.com/article/amazon-now-quick-commerce-blinkit-swiggy-instamart-zepto-XSr8g1JWxwreEr19

Current rules on Gold funds and ETFs is like this: they have to buy LBMA gold, so any new ETF purchase will import gold from abroad.

SEBI should change the rules so that they can buy BIS gold (Indian certified gold) too so that recirculation in India will reduce imports.

items.title: RT abey varghese: “To be conscious is an illness,” Fyodor Dostoevsky’s protagonist says in Notes from Underground, the satirical novella he wrote i...
items.description_html:
RT abey varghese
“To be conscious is an illness,” Fyodor Dostoevsky’s protagonist says in Notes from Underground, the satirical novella he wrote in response to 19th-century Russian nihilism. “I have many times tried to become an insect. But I was not equal even to that.” In 21st-century India, however, the roach is not a metaphor for running away from problems. Instead, it’s a symbol of survival — in the swamps of communalized politics, corruption, and staggering bureaucratic callousness, writes @andymukherjee70. https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2026-06-03/india-s-cockroach-army-and-the-great-exam-betrayal?utm_source=website&utm_medium=share&utm_campaign=twitter

items.title: RT The Core: The RBI is more likely to explore ways to attract capital inflows than to tinker with the interest rates to take care of currency volatil...
items.description_html:
RT The Core
The RBI is more likely to explore ways to attract capital inflows than to tinker with the interest rates to take care of currency volatility, Chief Economist Rajani Sinha of @CareEdge_Group tells @govindethiraj on The Core Report.

"If the West Asia crisis lingers, we would definitely have an impact on growth. So the central bank has this difficult task of balancing inflation as well as taking care of the growth aspect."

The RBI’s Monetary Policy Committee will announce its decision on rates tomorrow.

👉 Key insights from the expert:

• Uncertainty surrounding the West Asia crisis and its impact on energy supplies will continue to impact financial markets, businesses, and consumer sentiment.

• Concerns over a weaker monsoon and its impact on food prices have heightened inflation risks.

• Raising interest rates is unlikely to significantly support the currency as the weak capital flows remain the core issue.

• FII outflows are common during crisis periods, but unlike before, the capital account was supported by stable FDI flows.

items.description_html:
Introducing The Roundup - Data For India’s new monthly newsletter!

Over the last couple of years, we’ve published dozens of in-depth analyses, built hundreds of new charts and built features to make our work easy to discover, share and reuse. But if you’re not checking the platform regularly, it might be easy to miss what’s new.

The Roundup is our way of keeping you in the loop, a simple monthly note that brings together:
📝 The latest work we’ve published
🖥️ New platform features and updates
📊 One chart that our team thought you shouldn't miss

Issue #1 is out now; every new issue will land in your inbox on the last Friday of every month.

Read the first issue and subscribe here: https://www.dataforindia.com/the-roundup/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

🚨 SoftBank Vision Fund has sold a 3.2% stake in Lenskart for $300 mn (Rs 2,870 cr) through a block deal on Wednesday.

This takes SoftBank’s total cash-out from Lenskart to more than $500 mn.

The Japanese investment firm invested $187 million and still owns nearly a 10% stake, currently worth about $940 mn (Rs 9,000 cr).

This implies total returns of nearly 8x, making Lenskart one of Masayoshi Son’s best-performing investments in India.

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: CLSA on Kotak BK O-P. TP Rs 440 Management meet takeaways Kotak has identified four customer cohorts that it would like to focus on –...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
CLSA on Kotak BK
O-P. TP Rs 440
Management meet takeaways
Kotak has identified four customer cohorts that it would like to focus on – affluent, middle-class, SME and corporate.
One change that has been brought about in bank is migration from product-level focus to customer-level focus
Idea is to look at a customer by the overall profitability they generate and not by a singular product approach.
Focus on technology clearly stood out, with an aim to reduce the cost of doing business over time.
While bank has reignited its focus on digital customers (‘811’), it will also focus on branch expansion
Felt that management was unwilling to sacrifice RoA for growth, despite its excess capital. It reiterated its usual loan growth guidance of 1.5-2x of nominal GDP.

UBS on Kotak BK
Buy TP Rs 500
Management meet takeaways
Focus on low-cost liability, retail loans and tech
Liability side to be supported by digital and branch
Tech to drive cost optimisation
On asset side, company continues to target 1.5-2x of nominal GDP growth with lower end likely in a weaker cycle & pursuing inorganic opportunities if required
Co would prioritise retail and MSME segments while remaining opportunistic in corporate loans & aspires to become third largest bank by group profit by 2030
@CNBCTV18Live @_RituSingh

items.title: Another comeback that is taking place: Blackberry BlackBerry is back. Not with a phone. Not with a keyboard. With an OS quietly running inside 275 mil...
items.description_html:
Another comeback that is taking place: Blackberry

BlackBerry is back. Not with a phone. Not with a keyboard. With an OS quietly running inside 275 million cars, humanoid robots, and surgical systems and now partnered with NVIDIA for the age of Physical AI. Here's the full story. 👇

2. In 2008, BlackBerry was worth ~$75 billion that is more than NVIDIA, Amazon, Netflix, and Starbucks combined at that time. It owned 50% of the US smartphone market. The "CrackBerry" was the status symbol of the era. Obama refused to give his up. Wall Street ran on it.

3. Then came the iPhone. And BlackBerry's co-CEOs did something fatal, they called the touchscreen "a gimmick." They doubled down on the physical keyboard and enterprise email while the world moved on. They missed touchscreen UX, failed to build an app ecosystem, and prioritized enterprise over a consumer revolution that was happening right in front of them.

4. The fall was brutal. By 2016, BlackBerry's smartphone market share was literally 0.0%. Revenue cratered, the stock fell 96% from its peak, and by 2018 they had exited the phone business entirely. Most investors wrote the obituary and moved on.

5. What almost everyone missed: buried inside this wreck of a phone company was a software gem they'd quietly acquired in 2010 — QNX. For years it sat ignored while the phone business burned. But QNX was doing something remarkable: becoming the nervous system of modern automobiles.

6. QNX is a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) — the foundational software layer for safety-critical systems where failure is not an option. The touchscreen in your car runs Android Automotive. But when your car senses danger at 120 km/h and fires the emergency brakes? That's QNX. It's not the face. It's the backbone.

7. The moat has three layers. First — regulatory lock-in. QNX OS for Safety is certified to ISO 26262 ASIL-D, the absolute highest functional safety standard in automotive. Getting a competitor to this level takes years and hundreds of millions. It's not just a technical moat, it's a regulatory moat. Apple tried to build a competing car OS, spent $10 billion, and quietly killed the project. That tells you everything about the barrier to entry.

8. Second layer is the automotive adoption cycles. When an OEM bakes QNX into a vehicle platform, it stays for the life of that platform which is often 7 to 10 years. Switching costs are astronomical. Recertification from scratch is unthinkable. BMW just selected QNX for its next-generation Neue Klasse platform. That's a decade of locked, recurring revenue from one design win.

9. Third layer is 45 years of safety-critical DNA. QNX was founded in 1980. Its microkernel architecture, where safety and non-critical processes are completely memory-isolated, took decades to perfect. This isn't software you build in a sprint. It's institutional knowledge accumulated over nearly half a century.

10. The metric that matters most is the royalty backlog — locked future revenue from already-signed contracts. In FY2022 it stood at $460M Today it's ~$950M. A 2x jump in four years, providing revenue visibility. This is predictable, high-margin, recurring software revenue and the market is still waking up to it.

11. Now comes the AI angle most investors are sleeping on. The world is moving fast toward what QNX calls "Physical AI" machines that don't just compute, they perceive, reason, and act in the real world. Humanoid robots. Surgical systems. Industrial automation. Autonomous vehicles. Every single one needs software that is deterministic, real-time, and safety-certified. That's QNX's exact domain.

12. The NVIDIA partnership is the key signal. In August 2025, QNX integrated with NVIDIA DRIVE AGX Thor for autonomous vehicles. Then in April 2026 at Hannover Messe, they deepened it, QNX OS for Safety 8.0 + NVIDIA IGX Thor + Halos Safety Stack for targeting robotics, medical tech, and industrial automation. BB stock jumped 11% on the TSX the day of the announcement. NVIDIA is the dominant AI hardware platform. QNX is now its safety OS layer. Think Windows for physical AI.

13. Already deployed across 24 of the world's top 25 EV manufacturers. Inside BMW, Mercedes, Audi, Toyota, Tata, and Mahindra. Demonstrated inside humanoid robots at Embedded World 2026 and surgical robotic arms at CES 2026. About 20% of QNX revenue now comes from non-automotive sectors and that share is growing fast.

14. This isn't just a BlackBerry story. There's a mega-theme in 2026: dot-com era giants are having AI-era revivals. Cisco went from $800M in AI orders to $9B projected in FY2026, and its stock surged 17% in a single day, its best day since 2002. Intel, Dell, and Corning are all seeing the same dynamic.

The companies that built the original infrastructure of the internet are now becoming the infrastructure of the AI age.

15. Q4 FY2026 numbers confirm the turnaround. Total revenue hit $156M (+10% YoY). QNX revenue: $78.7M (+20% YoY). Full-year QNX revenue: $268M (+14%). Gross margin: 78.2% and expanding. Adjusted EBITDA for the full year: $107.1M (+27%). Operating cash flow: $45.6M

GAAP net income: $53.2M which is the 8th consecutive quarter of improving profitability.

The royalty backlog: ~$950M. The CEO said it plainly: "The turnaround is complete."

16. FY2027 guidance: revenue of $584M–$611M, QNX alone at $290M–$307M. The company sits on $432M in cash with a share buyback authorized. Key growth triggers: QNX Alloy Core (won CES 2026 Best in Show), BMW Neue Klasse ramp, robotics pipeline with Boston Dynamics and Agility Robotics, NATO-accredited government contracts, and a growing developer ecosystem with 80 universities building on QNX globally.

17. The final word. "On a car, you'll never see QNX's logo. What you will see is a better experience." That invisibility is the thesis. The best infrastructure businesses don't scream for attention. They become indispensable. QNX is the TCP/IP of physical AI.

Not investment advice. Do your own research.

items.title: 1 GW greenfield nuclear power plant requires 4,000–5,000 tons of pipe spools. The current pipeline of plants both domestically and internationally is...
items.description_html:
1 GW greenfield nuclear power plant requires 4,000–5,000 tons of pipe spools.

The current pipeline of plants both domestically and internationally is large enough that RFSS would stay booked just on new greenfield projects alone, without needing replacement demand to sustain volumes.

2047 vision of 100 GW of nuclear capacity as the long-term demand driver.

To meet the broader power demand thermal and renewable alone won't suffice, especially with the data center buildout accelerating globally.

RFSS is currently the only NPCIL-approved pipe spooling facility from India. This is a meaningful competitive moat given nuclear qualification timelines. Beyond NPCIL, they've now secured international approvals for nuclear projects in Egypt, Turkey, and Hungary expanding the TAM beyond domestic.

Current RFSS order book stands at ~550 crores, with ₹480–500 crores expected to convert into revenue in FY27 itself (guidance: 20–25% growth over FY26s 390 crores). The active bidding pipeline is $400–500 million

Pipe spool orders are placed roughly 1–1.5 years after land acquisition for a nuclear plant. Delivery timelines range from 6 to 18 months post-order. So from land acquisition to spool delivery, you're looking at a 1.5–3 year lag

Current RFSS margins are running at 30–35%. Sanghvi guided that near-term margins look sustainable at these levels, but over time should normalize to 20–25% as the business scales and competition potentially enters.

Disclaimer - not a buy/sell recommendation

items.title: Which battery chemicals will be required as Battery production scales up China’s EV battery demand rose sharply from around 16GWh in 2015 to nearly 7...
items.description_html:
Which battery chemicals will be required as Battery production scales up

China’s EV battery demand rose sharply from around 16GWh in 2015 to nearly 770GWh by 2025, showing how quickly demand can scale once electric vehicle or EV penetration accelerates.

If India follows a similar path with a lag, battery demand could reach around 200GWh by 2032F.

This would create demand for nearly 300,000t of cathode material, 180,000t of anode material, 200,000–240,000t of electrolytes, 26,000–36,000t of LiPF₆ and 160,000–200,000t of solvents.

items.title: Ferrochrome situation in SA - South Africa negotiated a 62 cents/kWh power tariff for ferrochrome down from 136 cents standard rate. Will this restart...
items.description_html:
Ferrochrome situation in SA -

South Africa negotiated a 62 cents/kWh power tariff for ferrochrome down from 136 cents standard rate.

Will this restart SA production and flood the market? Unlikely.

More SA ferrochrome → less chrome ore to China → lower Chinese production. Chrome units stay broadly neutral globally.

The real question isn't if SA restarts it's whether 62c is sustainable long-term. Glencore says it's survival pricing. If ferrochrome prices crack, even this tariff falls apart.

Ultimate resilience is not shaped through policy interventions but through strong fundamentals.

Source - IMFA Q4 Concall

Disclaimer - not a recommendation to buy/sell

items.title: Shankar bhai, net imports is the only way to judge reserves, and let me explain why. We pay for imports with a lag, and we receive export proceeds wit...
items.description_html:
Shankar bhai, net imports is the only way to judge reserves, and let me explain why. We pay for imports with a lag, and we receive export proceeds with a lag. Assuming that we will not get any export proceeds, while having to pay for imports is extreme overkill.

Also remember that a lot of imports are used to then export. Which means we would have imported some crude oil to refine and send out petrol/diesel. This happens every month, and payments and receipts happen every month. Assuming exports stop, then that means corresponding imports also stop suddenly and do not need to be paid for, and whatever imports are already in transit is going be paid for by whatever imports are in transit on the outward direction.

Similarly, exports of services happen and don't just stop. They are independent in some way.

The IMF methodology is wrong. We all know it, because of how different it works in reality, even in a crisis. And we have seen many crisis areas, net imports are the only real mechanism on which to judge reserves, not gross. The dogma that oh my god IMF is right because, well because it's IMF, is very wrong.

In the simple example, things dont happen in a vacuum. You don't import first and then export. You export things you make or haev already, including services. At the same time that you're importing. The practical example is that if you import $100 in a month and export $50 on what you already have produced, then you get the $50 at about the same time that you have to pay the $100, so you just need $50. At a country level, you do not assume that every single export fails to receive anything, while every single import has to be paid in full. It just is not practical.

And no, we didn't sell gold. And we've sold enough of our TBonds, and will continue to if FPIs keep selling.


Shankar Sharma: Owing to the limits on word count, a few important things were left out from the original version:

What most people don't know probably is that our FDI is not the way it is perceived: around 70-75% of it is VC PE money!

By no means can that be called stable capital . It is

items.title: RT Rajeev Mantri: Indian business is controlled by a small coterie of business dynasties, many of whom are linked together by marriage. It is a closed...
items.description_html:
RT Rajeev Mantri
Indian business is controlled by a small coterie of business dynasties, many of whom are linked together by marriage. It is a closed circle of elites, who function like old school zamindars.

One simply doesn’t expect these 21st century zamindars to be able to contribute to Indian economy’s growth to $20T in output. In fact, the Licence Raj orientation and manipulative, predatory mindset of many companies is a serious impediment to India’s progress.

For India to progress, these businesses have to be out-competed and neutered. The same had happened 30 years ago, when the first flush of reforms speared through and reshaped the Indian business landscape. Many of today’s corporate giants swept away blue chip names of that era - as life goes, today the challengers of that period have become like the old guard they had displaced.

The problem is barriers to entry and barriers to scaling slow down the turnover. This generation of Indians is counting on Prime Minister @narendramodi to deliver on necessary reforms, so that new entrants can once again displace the distracted, tired and corpulent incumbents who at this point can be generously described as squatters impeding the path of India’s progress. https://x.com/rmantri/status/2054118137986269360?s=46&t=N3l76pbsGLWSFxlrfMXdmQ


Uday Kotak: Google which is cash surplus, just announced an additional capital raise of $80 bn.
Google annual profit is $160 bn, last quarter $62 bn, and market cap $4.5 trillion. That is close to total profits and market cap of all Indian listed companies put together.
It’s a wake up call

items.title: Index funds in the US have to buy SpaceX, as nasdaq prepares to add it to the Nasdaq 100 in 15 days after listing, around July 7. They changed the rul...
items.description_html:
Index funds in the US have to buy SpaceX, as nasdaq prepares to add it to the Nasdaq 100 in 15 days after listing, around July 7. They changed the rules to allow fast track listing of any co thats in the top 40 by full market cap (usually 3 months, and nasdaq doesn't do free float, it uses full market Cap)

Two important changes to the index inclusion concept:

1. Companies with less than 10% free float can now list. This wasn't allowed earlier and spacex will have around 4% free float when it lists.

2. Normally a 3m curing period is needed formipps to be included. It's now just 15 trading days.

Both these allow Spacex to.get on to the Nasdaq index. The weight is limited to 3x it's free float which is like 0.5% of the index. This is not much at all to start with.

Other indexes too will eventually list the company. The free float will expand dramatically between 70 days and 180 days after listing, as shares get out of their lock in periods, at which time it's weight in the index will increase substantially.

The big deal will only be in that time frame. Every index rebalance, will increase spacex weight which means index funds will reduce other stocks and increase spacex exposure, just as shares get unlocked.

In some sense, this means that if we expect any damage due to spacex lockups opening, some of it will be absorbed by index funds and etfs. This helps the stock, of course, and perhaps the market.

Markets usually top with large ipos because the ipos suck out liquidity. That could happen but many months after the ipo, as unlocked investors sell and at the same time, spacex will need to raise more. It's an interesting thing that other ai companies too are planning to list soon. Perhaps they get it, and want to get their raises done before all this unfolds. Or perhaps it's just that they want in on this ride.

Given that passive is a substantial part of the market now, it's an easy choice to move the decision makers of indices. Think about it, a stock called pp waterballs could list at a market cap of 2 trillion with 4% free float, sold to friendly investment bankers, and between 15 days and 180 days, a large portion of the free float could be sold to index funds and index huggers.

Spacex might be revolutionary but it doesn't really have to be. Index creators are the new fund managers.

https://indexes.nasdaqomx.com/docs/Nasdaq-100_Index_Consultation_February_2026_Summary_of_Responses_and_Conclusion.pdf

Never ignore a business where:

1. Gross margins are expanding
2. Ebitda margins have declined due to pre opex
3. Historical track record is stellar
4. Company is doing pain today for gain tomorrow activity

These are unique set ups where investors discard these companies due to optically low profitability, and given the fact that these businesses are under earning.

Today's pain, ends up becoming tomorrow's gain when opex phase ends. Which is when we get operating leverage effect

items.title: Jeena Sikho Lifecare’s management has laid out an aggressive growth roadmap. Current sales: ~₹800 crore 3–5 year sales target: ~₹3,000 crore PAT t...
items.description_html:
Jeena Sikho Lifecare’s management has laid out an aggressive growth roadmap.

Current sales: ~₹800 crore
3–5 year sales target: ~₹3,000 crore
PAT target: 4x–5x in 4 years
Bed capacity target: 7,000–10,000 beds in 3–5 years

But the bigger trigger could be insurance.

Current revenue per occupied bed is around ₹4,650/day.
New insurance agreements are being signed at ~₹8,800–₹9,200 per bed/day.

Ayushman Yojana draft rates are also around ₹8,800–₹9,400/day.

That means higher insurance coverage can materially improve realization per bed.

Management also said wellness treatments are being covered under insurance, and the company is in talks with insurers to route select disease cases to Jeena Sikho, where treatment can be delivered more cost-effectively.

The model is simple:

More beds + higher insurance coverage + recurring medicine revenue = operating leverage.

Execution will decide the story.


Beat The Street: Jeena Sikho Lifecare’s unit economics look interesting

A 100-bed facility typically needs ₹2.5–3.5 cr capex — just ₹3–4 lakh per bed.

Monthly fixed cost includes ₹50–55 lakh payroll + ₹10–12 lakh rent.

At ₹8,300/day/bed bundled Panchakarma revenue, a 100-bed centre can

items.title: RT SOIC Research: Today, let's talk about a SaaS business that most people would dismiss at first glance. An IT company. A software business. In 2025-...
items.description_html:
RT SOIC Research
Today, let's talk about a SaaS business that most people would dismiss at first glance.

An IT company. A software business. In 2025-26, the automatic reaction is, "AI will eat these guys alive."
And honestly? For most SaaS companies, that fear is valid.

But this one is different.

This isn't a generic software vendor selling dashboards or CRM tools. This business has quietly built itself into the invisible infrastructure of a $1.5 trillion global travel industry.

Every time you book a hotel on Booking. com, every time an airline adjusts its fare, every time a hotel decides how much to spend on Google ads there's a high chance this company's technology is running in the background, making that decision happen.

And here's the contrarian thesis that we'll break down in this thread:

AI doesn't disrupt this business. AI makes its moat deeper.

While the market debates whether SaaS companies will survive the AI wave, this one is already deploying AI agents that deliver 300% revenue lifts for customers, has built the world's largest travel intent data platform with 1.5 billion data points, and is in active conversations with OpenAI and Google to become the advertising infrastructure layer for AI-powered travel.

So in this thread, we're going to break it all down for you in simple terms:

→ What the business actually does (and why it can't be replicated)
→ Why financial metrics of this company are among the best in Indian SaaS
→ What's going to drive the next phase of growth
→ The real risks that you should watch
→ And the big question, is AI a threat or the biggest tailwind this business has ever had?

items.title: RT SOIC Research: Today, let's talk about a SaaS business that most people would dismiss at first glance. An IT company. A software business. In 2025-...
items.description_html:
RT SOIC Research
Today, let's talk about a SaaS business that most people would dismiss at first glance.

An IT company. A software business. In 2025-26, the automatic reaction is, "AI will eat these guys alive."
And honestly? For most SaaS companies, that fear is valid.

But this one is different.

This isn't a generic software vendor selling dashboards or CRM tools. This business has quietly built itself into the invisible infrastructure of a $1.5 trillion global travel industry.

Every time you book a hotel on Booking. com, every time an airline adjusts its fare, every time a hotel decides how much to spend on Google ads there's a high chance this company's technology is running in the background, making that decision happen.

And here's the contrarian thesis that we'll break down in this thread:

AI doesn't disrupt this business. AI makes its moat deeper.

While the market debates whether SaaS companies will survive the AI wave, this one is already deploying AI agents that deliver 300% revenue lifts for customers, has built the world's largest travel intent data platform with 1.5 billion data points, and is in active conversations with OpenAI and Google to become the advertising infrastructure layer for AI-powered travel.

So in this thread, we're going to break it all down for you in simple terms:

→ What the business actually does (and why it can't be replicated)
→ Why financial metrics of this company are among the best in Indian SaaS
→ What's going to drive the next phase of growth
→ The real risks that you should watch
→ And the big question, is AI a threat or the biggest tailwind this business has ever had?

items.title: RT Nithin Kamath: While everybody is busy stuffing an AI chatbot into every product, the folks at @Tijori1 are using AI to build high-quality market r...
items.description_html:
RT Nithin Kamath
While everybody is busy stuffing an AI chatbot into every product, the folks at @Tijori1 are using AI to build high-quality market research tools. They’re calling it Tijori Stack, and what they are doing is taking the messy and scattered universe of company disclosures, financials, filings, concalls, and regulatory documents, and extracting useful insights.

They have several products:

1. Concall Monitor

Tracking concalls is annoying because the transcript comes after a few days. Then there’s the fact that you have to sit through one hour of management-speak just to find the three useful things they said. Tijori is trying to fix that.

With Concall Monitor, a few minutes after a concall ends, you get the full transcript and an AI-generated summary that extracts the key insights like pricing, margins, growth, demand, guidance, capex, risks, etc.
But the interesting thing is not just summarization but the management consistency check. Tijori tracks what management has said historically vs. what they are saying now. If a company promised something four quarters ago, the system can help track whether they followed through, changed the story, postponed the target, or pretended the earlier comment never happened.

2. Report on Demand

Tijori ingests pretty much all the filings and disclosures that a company makes, from financials, concalls, exchange filings, and regulatory disclosures to other company-specific documents. Using this, they generate different kinds of company reports:

Risk Probe Report gives you all the red flags and key risks.

Management Credibility Report looks at what management said versus what they actually did.

Five-year revenue and EBITDA estimate is an AI-assisted financial forecast grounded in company data.

All these reports are useful when you are doing first-pass research on a company. If you are tracking five companies and want to quickly figure out which are bad and which are worth researching further, these reports help. Instead of reading annual reports, concalls, exchange filings, presentations, and financials from scratch, you can use these reports to build a baseline view in minutes.

3. Radar

Radar is probably my favourite idea in the stack.

It allows you to define a metric or risk you care about for a particular company, and Tijori keeps scanning company disclosures and financials for relevant mentions. When the metric you define shows up, you get an alert.

And the metric does not have to be a typical financial metric.

You can track things like client concentration, employee growth, dollar revenue exposure, margin pressure, working capital stress, promoter pledging, regulatory risk, capex delays, inventory build-up, receivables, raw material pressure, or anything else that matters to your thesis. This is powerful because every investor has a different question.

Radar turns those questions into live monitors. I don’t think there are many products in India, or even in the world, that do this well.

4. Atlas

When you use ChatGPT or Claude to ask questions about a listed company, the answer is based on info that scraped from the public web. This is problematic because public web data about companies is messy.

There is news, commentary, rumours, stale articles, bad research takes, and gossip. Sometimes it is useful, but often it is noise. What makes Atlas different is that it gives you answers that are grounded in company-specific material like filings, disclosures, financials, concalls, and other company data.

Instead of asking a generic chatbot, “Tell me about this company,” and getting an unreliable answer scraped from the internet, you are asking a system that is constrained by the company’s own disclosures and financial history. This ensures there are no hallucinations.

items.title: RT The Indian Express: The Maharashtra government on May 13 exempted an iron ore mining and processing project in Gadchiroli from wildlife clearance b...
items.description_html:
RT The Indian Express
The Maharashtra government on May 13 exempted an iron ore mining and processing project in Gadchiroli from wildlife clearance by claiming — incorrectly — it was not located in any tiger corridor.

The project proposal involves diverting 9.4 sq km of forest land in Gadchiroli for iron ore extraction and processing by Lloyds Metals & Energy.

It received forest clearance on April 15 and environment clearance on May 12, a day before it was exempted from obtaining wildlife clearance.

According to the project site map submitted by Lloyds to the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change, the area falls in forest compartments 196, 197, 273, 274, 275, 276, 298, 300 and 301.

All but one (300) of these compartments are identified as part of the Tadoba-Indravati tiger corridor in the National Tiger Conservation Authority (NTCA)-approved Tiger Conservation Plan (TCP) of the Tadoba Andhari tiger reserve.

items.description_html:
RT Ritesh Banglani
Continuing the theme from yesterday, it baffles me that our instinct to solve every overcrowding problem is to curtail demand rather than build capacity.

China has built 2000 km of railway in Tibet, of which 1000 km is above an unbelievable 13,000 feet (Joshimath is at less than half that altitude). Yet we are litigating a daily quota on tourists instead of demanding an expansion of transport infrastructure.

Instead of blaming the tourists for wanting to escape the intense heat of the Indian plains, we should be asking why our vast Himalaya cannot accommodate 10x the number of visitors than it does today.


Himalayan Hindu: The obsession with breaking records has successfully turned the fragile Himalayas into the world's longest PARKING LOT. Joshimath is currently choking under a massive 25-30 KM long traffic jam. From Vishnuprayag to 15 km beyond, tourists are stuck in their cars for hours.

India’s Presidency of BRICS has come at a very pivotal time for the global economy. It was in this context that the BRICS Academic Mid‑Term Conference in Dehradun, on 29 May 2026, assumed a lot of significance and enthusiastic participation from across the BRICS member countries plus partner nations.

CareEdge Global and the Observer Research Foundation launched an important new report at the Conference: “Breaking convention: How CareEdge Global Ratings is redefining global credit risk assessment.” The report — unveiled by MD and Group CEO of CareEdge - Mr. Mehul Pandya; Vice‑Chancellor, Doon University, Dehradun - Dr. Prof Surekha Dangwal, and Vice President - ORF and Executive Director, BRICS Think Tank Council - India, Dr. Harsh V. Pant — lays out a forward‑looking framework that challenges traditional credit paradigms and addresses geopolitical, climate, and structural risks shaping sovereign and corporate credit today. The event also featured an excellent keynote address by Shri Shambhu L. Hakki, Jt. Secretary (MER) and BRICS Sous-Sherpa for India.

A proud moment for collaboration between academia, policy, and industry. Read the report to understand the next frontier in credit risk thinking.

#CareEdgeGlobal #ORF #BRICS #SovereignRisk #CreditRatings

items.title: The availability of healthcare workers is a key measure of the effective functioning of a country's health system. It reflects not just access to care...
items.description_html:
The availability of healthcare workers is a key measure of the effective functioning of a country's health system. It reflects not just access to care, but also the capacity of the system to support its population.

India's Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW) records about 5.7 million healthcare professionals in India. This includes registered doctors, registered nurses and midwives, and registered AYUSH practitioners.

India's healthcare system also trains, recognises and employs AYUSH doctors, who are formally trained in traditional systems of medicine including ayurveda, unani, siddha, naturopathy, sowa rigpa (traditional medical system practised in the Himalayan region), and homeopathy. Nurses include not just registered nurses, but also Auxiliary Nurse Midwives (ANMs) and Lady Health Visitors (LHVs).

This amounts to about 41 registered healthcare professionals for every 10,000 people in India, including doctors, nurses and midwives. This is close to the World Health Organization's (WHO) suggested threshold of 44.5 per 10,000 people needed to meet healthcare-related sustainable development goals.

More than half of these professionals are nurses and midwives. For every ten doctors, there are roughly 17 nurses and midwives. The number of nurses has grown faster than the number of doctors, almost doubling between 2012 and 2023.

However, despite nearing the benchmark, the distribution of health professionals remains uneven across states and between urban and rural areas.

How does the distribution of registered healthcare professionals look in different regions across India? Read @nileena_suresh's analysis to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/health-workers/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Health #Doctor #Nurse #PublicHealth #India #DataForIndia

items.description_html:
RT ANI
#WATCH | Mumbai, Maharashtra: National Stock Exchange of India (NSE) Managing Director and CEO Ashishkumar Chauhan attends the CareEdge Debt Market Summit 2026.

He says, “There is an old saying among bridge builders: the structure is only as strong as its weakest pillar. India's financial architecture has built an impressive equity market infrastructure over the last three and a half decades. The corporate bond market is the pillar that must now be strengthened to the same standard…The question before us is how far we have progressed and how much further we need to go. The progress is real and measurable. India's outstanding corporate bond market has expanded at an annualised pace of 13.6% to ₹59.1 lakh crore in Financial Year 2026, growing faster than the overall bank credit growth of 12.1% over the same period. As a share of GDP, corporate bonds now stand at 17.1%, up from 11.7% in Financial Year 2012… Corporate bonds represent 21.5% of the overall domestic debt market. It's meaningful, but not yet transformative…”

items.title: India is witnessing an exponential growth in digital payments, propelled by rising mobile phone and internet access. Aside from cash, more than 95% of...
items.description_html:
India is witnessing an exponential growth in digital payments, propelled by rising mobile phone and internet access.

Aside from cash, more than 95% of the money spent every day on major retail payments was made using cheques just two decades ago. However, since the introduction of the National Electronic Fund Transfer (NEFT) in 2005-06, and later with the rise of Unified Payments Interface (UPI), digital retail payments have come to dominate, according to statistics maintained by the Reserve Bank of India.

In the last five years, the use of UPI has in particular grown dramatically. The average daily number of UPI transactions grew from 40 million per day in 2019 to 550 million per day in 2025, outstripping all other modes of digital payments.

The largest daily transactions by value, on the other hand, take place via NEFT, despite being much fewer in number. From 2020 to January 2025, the average daily transaction value on NEFT doubled from Rs 600 billion to Rs 1.2 trillion.

UPI is mainly used for small peer-to-peer and peer-to-merchant payments, while NEFT is used for transactions with a larger ticket size. The average value of a UPI transaction is around Rs 1,500, and for an NEFT transaction is a little under Rs 50,000.

Has this progress in retail digital payments led to equitable access to financial services across India? Read @akwaghmare 's piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/retail-payments/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Payments #Banking #Finance #India #DataForIndia

items.title: I hosted @kanodiaankit12 on the podcast again. Last time we spoke, most micro caps were bleeding. This time, we went somewhere I didn't expect - a ful...
items.description_html:
I hosted @kanodiaankit12 on the podcast again.

Last time we spoke, most micro caps were bleeding. This time, we went somewhere I didn't expect - a full breakdown of consumption as a sector.

Food. Retail. Cinema. Amusement parks. Luggage. Hotels.
45 minutes of pure insights.

But the thing that hit me hardest had nothing to do with stock picks.

He said: "You're not an allocator of money. You're first an allocator of time."
Every investable rupee he has goes into equity. No FDs. No debt funds. Because he's clear he's still in asset creation mode.

His portfolio? 15 stocks max. His co-founder runs 5.

Concentrated. Intentional. Not 50 stocks dressed up as "diversification."
Most people in their 20s and 30s obsess over asset allocation. That's a preservation mindset. You can't preserve what you haven't built yet.

He also admitted the market has humbled him on stocks he's held for 3 years. No spin.

Even said he's considering selling one position just to see how he feels about the business without owning it.

That kind of self-awareness is rare.

Watch our full conversation here - https://youtu.be/TasQSPIhg08?si=9Kf6cDMVaGB3Kyb2

items.title: Indian households use a range of cooking fuels, including Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG), natural gas (primarily Piped Natural Gas or PNG), firewood, o...
items.description_html:
Indian households use a range of cooking fuels, including Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG), natural gas (primarily Piped Natural Gas or PNG), firewood, other natural sources (primarily dung cake), and electricity. Some households also report having no cooking arrangement.

The use of LPG as cooking fuel across Indian households has grown steadily over time. In 2000, fewer than 20% of households used LPG, with the majority reliant on firewood. By the late 2010s, the share using LPG had overtaken the share using firewood, and it continues to grow.

As of 2024, six in ten Indian households use LPG as their primary cooking fuel. A third of Indian households still rely on firewood.

Firewood use is concentrated in rural households. In urban areas, more than four in five households now use LPG. The share using natural gas, including PNG, remains small and geographically concentrated.

This data comes from India’s Household Consumption Expenditure Survey, which asks the head of the household about its primary energy source for cooking in the 30 days preceding the survey.

How does LPG usage vary across different regions in India? Read @Rukmini’s analysis to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/cooking-fuel/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#LPG #Fuel #Cooking #India #DataForIndia

items.title: One of the most important measures of a country's nutritional and public health status is whether its children are adequately nourished. The most clos...
items.description_html:
One of the most important measures of a country's nutritional and public health status is whether its children are adequately nourished. The most closely tracked indicator on child nutrition outcomes is stunting.

A child is considered stunted if their height is more than two standard deviations lower than the median for children of the same age and gender, using World Health Organization growth standards.

In 1993, more than one in two children under the age of five in India was stunted. By 2022, this rate dropped to one in three, indicating a decline in stunting over time. Even then, the levels of child stunting in India remain high and are comparable to those seen in Sub-Saharan African nations with much lower per capita incomes.

Stunting affects 30% of urban children, while the rate is higher in rural areas at 37%. Across both sectors, children from the richest households are about half as likely to be stunted compared to those from the poorest households.

These persistently high rates of stunting in India despite economic growth have led to some debate around the WHO's international growth standards. However, research suggests that the high rates of stunting in India reflect a combination of multiple underlying factors, rather than just issues with the standards themselves.

How does stunting affect children from different regions across India? Read @nileena_suresh's analysis to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/child-stunting/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Health #PublicHealth #Child #Stunting #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Agentic routing is such an interesting study. Built a basic agent that does planning on Opus 4 but reasons on 4.6 through a tool call (skill call). Du...
items.description_html: Agentic routing is such an interesting study. Built a basic agent that does planning on Opus 4 but reasons on 4.6 through a tool call (skill call). Due to lack of document grounding, I embedded corpus into system prompt. Worked well. Second stage, enabled managed RAG for grounding & agent stated skipping tool call entirely, no skill calls. It treated grounded data as a higher evidence deterministic choice & skipped skill call. Tweaked tool description, still no skill call. Changed skill description matching to tool call & bingo!! Agent skipped grounding, entered planning mode & made a tool call. Evals for Agentic behavior is literally out paces everything in this field. What's your such key learning from a recent build?

items.title: Access to safe, usable toilet facilities is a critical component of public health and safety. India has long struggled with very low levels of sanitat...
items.description_html:
Access to safe, usable toilet facilities is a critical component of public health and safety.

India has long struggled with very low levels of sanitation, significantly worse than many comparable countries. In 2000, only 14% of India’s population had access to at least a basic sanitation facility, placing it behind much poorer countries in Sub-Saharan Africa, such as Somalia. When people do not have access to a safe and usable toilet, they are forced to defecate in the open.

However, over the next two decades, India made consistent progress in improving access to sanitation. By 2022, India had surpassed countries that were at a similar level in 2000, or were ahead at the time.

Basic sanitation facilities are defined as improved sanitation facilities that are not shared with other households. These include flush/pour-flush to piped sewer systems, septic tanks or pit latrines; ventilated improved pit latrines, composting toilets or pit latrines with slabs. This categorisation is based on WHO and UNICEF's definition.

What kind of toilet facilities do Indian households have access to? Read @nileena_suresh's piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/sanitation/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Toilets #Sanitation #WASH #India #DataForIndia

items.title: A lot of defense to IT SIs right now is based on assumption that enterprise clients will not open their systems, data & processes to external AI solut...
items.description_html: A lot of defense to IT SIs right now is based on assumption that enterprise clients will not open their systems, data & processes to external AI solutions (Claude, OpenAi). However, this defense collapses the moment we have a precedence from a big enterprise opening up just the way enterprises opened up to cloud after a decade long friction. The moment this starts happening, the JVs OpenAi & Claude have formed will make a killing. And, what will take this wall down? For e.g., enterprises are currently very upset after SAP API policy that prohibited clients from using their own data & sending it to external tools via APIs to architect a solution outside of SAP. This can be the 1st hammer in the wall. One wrong step & clients will find ways to disconnect with enterprise OEMs & will be willing to trust(with an agreement perhaps) likes of Claude & OpenAi. The moment this happens, it is done!! This is the biggest risk I foresee.

items.description_html:
Higher education is seen as a key to getting young Indians into skilled, high-paying jobs and accelerating economic growth.

Just under 40 million young people (aged 18-23) were enrolled in higher education programmes in India as of 2022. This included both undergraduate (or equivalent) and postgraduate (or equivalent) levels of study.

However, at 32%, enrolment in tertiary or higher education in India is still below the world average.

In the early 1990s, India’s higher education enrolment rates were similar to China’s, for instance. But in the two decades since, China has made big strides: more than seven in ten young Chinese adults are now in higher education, compared to three in ten young Indian adults.

How does higher education enrolment look across states, and what disciplines do students choose to study in India? Read @akwaghmare’s piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/higher-education/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post
